speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. Let's start by checking your vitals — your diastolic blood pressure today is 66 mmHg.

speaker: patient
text: Hi, thanks for seeing me. I’ve been feeling a bit off lately — my heart’s been racing sometimes, especially in the afternoons.

speaker: patient
text: Yeah, palpitations — like my heart’s skipping or just pounding out of my chest.

speaker: doctor
text: I see. How long have you been noticing that? Any triggers — stress, caffeine, lying down?

speaker: patient
text: It started about three days ago. I cut back on coffee, but it still happens — even when I’m resting.

speaker: doctor
text: Okay, let’s check your blood pressure again. Hmm — systolic is now 156 mmHg. That’s elevated, though diastolic remains low at 66.

speaker: doctor
text: Your systolic blood pressure today is 156 mmHg.

speaker: doctor
text: You’re currently on Metformin 1000 mg twice daily for type 2 diabetes — correct?

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your persistent elevated readings and absence of secondary causes, I’m diagnosing essential hypertension.

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin level is 171 g/L — within normal range, no anemia concerns.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your BP pattern and symptoms, I’m going to switch you from no antihypertensive to Amlodipine 5 mg once daily.

speaker: doctor
text: Just rechecked — your diastolic pressure is now 81 mmHg, which is higher than the 66 we saw earlier.

speaker: patient
text: Oh, really? I thought it was supposed to go down with medication — is that normal?

speaker: doctor
text: It can fluctuate — especially with white-coat effect or timing. But let’s monitor closely. Did you take your Metformin this morning?

speaker: doctor
text: Wait — this reading shows systolic at 180 mmHg. That’s significantly higher than the 156 we saw earlier. Any anxiety just now?

speaker: patient
text: I did feel nervous walking in — but honestly, my home cuff gave me 66 for the bottom number this morning too, same as your first reading.

speaker: patient
text: That reading — the 66 — matches what you got when I first sat down. It’s consistent for me at home.

speaker: doctor
text: Interesting — so you're seeing low diastolic with variable systolic. We’ll need ambulatory monitoring to assess for possible wide pulse pressure or autonomic influence.

speaker: patient
text: Makes sense. I’ve also noticed dizziness when standing up fast — could that relate?

speaker: patient
text: And that 156 top number you measured earlier — it’s the same one I got on my wrist monitor yesterday afternoon.
